mnemonics.title: CAP Theorem
mnemonics.content: CAP = Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance - Remember: 'Choose A Partition' - when partition occurs, you must choose between Consistency or Availability. CA (not possible with partitions), CP (consistent but unavailable), AP (available but may be stale).

mnemonics.title: BASE Properties
mnemonics.content: BASE = Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent - Remember: 'B'ASE as opposed to 'A'CID. Think of BASE like a 'base' foundation that allows flexibility vs rigid ACID rules.

mnemonics.title: NoSQL Types
mnemonics.content: 4 Types: Document (stores docs), Key-Value (simple pairs), Column-Family (wide columns), Graph (nodes + edges). Memory: 'Do Kee Col Gra' - Document Key Column Graph.

mnemonics.title: NoSQL Advantages
mnemonics.content: NoSQL Benefits: Flexible Schema, Horizontal Scaling, High Performance, Cloud-Ready, Schema-on-Read. Memory: 'FHPSC' = Flexible, Horizontal, Performance, Scalable, Cloud.

acronyms.usage: Term for non-relational, distributed databases that don't use traditional SQL/table structure.
Fundamental theorem describing tradeoffs in distributed database systems.

acronyms.expansion: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent
acronyms.usage: Transaction model used by NoSQL databases as alternative to ACID.
Traditional database transaction properties (NoSQL often trades these for scalability).
Binary-encoded serialization format used by MongoDB for storing documents.
Traditional SQL-based databases with fixed schemas (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL).

visualTips.visualization: Draw a triangle with 'Consistency' at top, 'Availability' bottom-left, 'Partition Tolerance' bottom-right. Show that during partitions, you can only maintain 2 vertices (choose CP or AP, PT is mandatory).
Use a tree diagram: Root = NoSQL, Branches = Document (MongoDB icon), Key-Value (key icon), Column-Family (spreadsheet icon), Graph (network nodes). Each has distinct data model visualization.
Draw horizontal timeline showing: Write happens at T1 → Different replicas show different values T2-T3 → All replicas converge to same value at T4. Shows 'eventual' nature.
Side-by-side diagrams: Vertical = Single tower getting taller (more power), Horizontal = Multiple small servers connected (more nodes). NoSQL prefers horizontal.

analogies.analogy: Like a filing cabinet where each folder can contain different documents - one folder has forms, another has photos, another has letters. Unlike rigid drawers where all must be same type.

Think of a bank during network outage: Either ATMs show 'unavailable' (maintain consistency - CP) or they show available but potentially wrong balance (maintain availability - AP). Can't have both during outage.
Like updating status on multiple social media apps - your post appears instantly on your phone, takes 5 minutes on desktop, 10 minutes on tablet, but eventually all show the same update.
Like a dictionary/lookup book - you give the word (key), you get the definition (value). Extremely fast for finding exact matches but can't search by partial matches.
Like a social network web - people are nodes (circles), relationships are edges (lines connecting circles). Easy to find 'friends of friends' because connections are explicit.
